_meta.title: The Compilation Pipeline End To End

questions.question: In the compilation pipeline, what is the primary output of the lexer stage?
options: An abstract syntax tree
A stream of tokens
Intermediate representation
Machine code
questions.explanation: The lexer (scanner) converts a character stream into a token stream. Each token is a categorized chunk of text such as a keyword, identifier, number, or operator.

questions.question: What is the key advantage of separating a compiler into a frontend and a backend?
options: It eliminates the need for an optimizer
It allows N source languages and M targets to share N + M components instead of N × M
It removes the need for a linker
It guarantees single-pass compilation
questions.explanation: With frontend/backend separation, each language needs only a frontend and each target needs only a backend. LLVM exemplifies this: Clang (C/C++), rustc (Rust), and Swift all emit LLVM IR, which then targets x86, ARM, RISC-V, etc.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes LLVM IR?
options: A high-level scripting language for build automation
A typed, SSA-based, RISC-like intermediate representation used as a universal middle layer
A binary object file format for linking
A preprocessor macro language
questions.explanation: LLVM IR is a typed, static single assignment (SSA) based intermediate representation with a RISC-like instruction set. It serves as the contract between language frontends and architecture backends.

questions.question: What distinguishes a JIT compiler from a traditional ahead-of-time (AOT) compiler?
options: JIT compilers do not use an AST
JIT compilers compile code at runtime, often starting with interpretation and compiling hot paths to machine code
JIT compilers cannot perform optimization
JIT compilers produce object files for later linking
questions.explanation: A JIT compiler executes code initially via interpretation or baseline compilation, profiles which code paths are hot (frequently executed), and then compiles those paths to optimized machine code at runtime.

questions.question: What are the major phases of a typical compilation pipeline?
options: Edit, save, run, debug
Lexing, parsing, semantic analysis, optimization, code generation
Compile, link, load, execute
Input, output, error handling
questions.explanation: A compiler transforms source code through: (1) Lexing — tokenizing input, (2) Parsing — building an AST, (3) Semantic analysis — type checking, scope resolution, (4) Optimization — improving the IR, (5) Code generation — emitting target code. Linking and loading come after compilation.
